key_points: The Revolution represented a transition from 'Absolutism' to 'Popular Sovereignty,' shifting the source of power from God/Monarch to the People.
The 'Great Fear' (July-August 1789) was a socio-political phenomenon where rumors of 'brigands' hired by aristocrats led to a preemptive peasant uprising that broke the back of the feudal system.
The Civil Constitution of the Clergy (1790) turned the Catholic Church into a department of the state, causing a deep religious schism that alienated many devout peasants from the Revolution.
The Flight to Varennes (June 1791) was the turning point where Louis XVI lost all public trust, proving he was a 'traitor' to the Revolution and making a Republic inevitable.
The Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen established 'Universalism'—the idea that rights are inherent to all humans, though in practice, it excluded women and colonial subjects initially.
The Thermidorian Reaction (1794) was a conservative turn that dismantled the planned economy (Price Caps/Maximum) of the Jacobins, favoring the wealthy bourgeoisie.
The rise of Napoleon was not an accident; it was a result of the 'Directory's' inability to provide executive stability, leading the French to trade political liberty for civil order.
The Revolution's legacy is dualistic: it birthed modern Liberal Democracy but also provided the first modern template for 'Totalitarianism' during the Terror.
historiography_and_theory: The Social Contract (Rousseau): The idea that the law is the expression of the 'General Will,' which became the ideological foundation for the Jacobin Republic.
The Bourgeoisie Revolution: The Marxist interpretation that 1789 was primarily the victory of the capitalist middle class over the feudal aristocracy.
Sovereignty: The shift of authority from the Crown to the Nation (a defined body of citizens with shared rights).
The Paradox of Liberty: How the Committee of Public Safety justified 'Terror' as a necessary tool to preserve 'Liberty' against internal and external enemies.
complex_examples: The Vendée Uprising: A massive counter-revolutionary civil war in western France sparked by religious changes and military conscription.
Olympe de Gouges: Her execution symbolized the Revolution's limits regarding gender equality and the 'General Will's' intolerance of dissent.
The Triangular Trade: The economic dependence of French ports like Bordeaux on Saint-Domingue (Haiti) created a moral contradiction for the National Assembly.
San-culottes: The radical urban working class (literally 'without knee-breeches') who pushed the Revolution toward more egalitarian and violent phases.
exam_tips: Analyze the impact of the 'Flight to Varennes' on the transition from the 1791 Constitution to the 1792 Republic.
Be prepared to discuss the 'Contradictions of 1789'—how the Declaration of Rights coexisted with the continuation of slavery and the exclusion of women.
Evaluate the Directory as a bridge between the radical Republic and the Napoleonic Empire; explain why it failed (Executive-Legislative deadlock).
Connect the 'Subsistence Crisis' not just to hunger, but to the 'Political Economy'—how the lack of grain led to a loss of faith in the King's 'paternal' protection.
Explain the global impact: Mention how Tipu Sultan and Raja Rammohan Roy in India were inspired by the ideas of 1789.
